node_2: social media interactions, paper citations, web links, ontologies
edge: Common properties of real-world networks include social media interactions, paper citations, web links, and ontologies.
Directed graphs have the property of transitivity. Transitivity in a directed graph means that if there exists a directed path from one vertex to another, then the edge between them also exists.
Directed graphs can exhibit cyclicity. In a directed graph, a cycle is a directed path that starts from a vertex and loops back to itself.
Organic matter is a part of the food cycle. For example, in a food cycle, 'organic matter' plays a role.
If 'cat is mammal' and 'mammal is animal' are true, then 'cat is animal' is also true. This demonstrates the concept of transitivity in directed graphs.

DeepWalk is a graph embedding method that maps each vertex to a vector in Euclidean space. This method is designed to capture important information from a graph using a differentiable parameterization.
Node2vec is another graph embedding method that maps vertices to vectors in Euclidean space. It is used to model undirected graphs and capture salient information from them.

Some methods for undirected graphs can be extended to capture edge asymmetry in directed graphs. This can be achieved by using separate representations for the source and target, which may or may not be related through a function.
Separate source and target vectors can encode any graph, including those with cycles. By using a dot product or distance-based energy function, these vectors can represent complex graph structures.
Relationship between an edge and directed paths where the encoding of relationships resulting from directed paths is harder when source and target representations live in separate spaces.
The vector model can learn to make relationships among vectors, but encoding transitivity, such as edge (i, k), is harder when source and target spaces are disconnected.
Demonstration of using GBC-BOX to represent a directed graph where interval embeddings and binary code vectors combine into box embeddings to represent a directed acyclic graph (DAG).

One limitation of Binary Code Box Embeddings is that the asymmetric transitivity might be weakened to model cycles with separate sub-spaces.
Another limitation of Binary Code Box Embeddings is the inefficiency of parameterization compared to a G-BOX model, leading to an increase in the number of parameters.
To address the limitations, a regularization method is introduced to penalize dimension drop-off using the lasso with a regularization weight wr.
Furthermore, another approach is to constrain the number of trainable binary codes to mitigate issues, leading to the introduction of Restricted Binary Code Size.
Both regularization method and Restricted Binary Code Size approach can provide a handle on mitigating issues mentioned above.
